messages.content: You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My wheat plants are looking weak and some are drying up, what's going on?

It sounds like termites might be the problem. They attack the roots and lower stems of wheat plants, causing yellowing, stunted growth, and plants that pull out easily. You can try treating the seeds before sowing with Chlorpyriphos 20% EC or thiomethoxam 30% FS. Also, make sure to keep the field clean and use well-decomposed manure. If the problem is already there, you can use Chlorpyrifos 20% EC with irrigation water. Be careful when using chemicals and follow the instructions closely.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My wheat plants are getting weak and dying, what's causing it?

Your wheat plants might be getting attacked by termites, which are also called deemak or geda. These pests live underground and damage the roots and stems of the plants. You might see yellowing leaves, stunted growth, and plants that can be pulled out easily. To manage them, you can use pesticides like Chlorpyrifos 20% EC or treat seeds with thiomethoxam. You can also use biopesticides like Beauvaria bassiana. Keep your field clean and avoid leaving crop residues. Make sure to follow safety rules when using chemicals.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My wheat plants are not growing well, and some are getting pulled out easily. What could be the problem?

It might be termite damage. Termites attack the roots and lower stem of wheat plants, causing them to dry up and be easily pulled out. This happens more in fields with lots of organic matter or near trees. To manage it, you can use chlorpyrifos or thiomethoxam for seed treatment, apply biopesticides like Beauveria bassiana, and keep the field clean by removing stubble and debris. Also, make sure to irrigate properly and avoid using fresh organic matter.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How do I stop termites from eating my wheat plants?

Termites can damage wheat plants by eating their roots. To stop them, treat the seeds with Chlorpyriphos 20% EC or thiomethoxam 30% FS before sowing. You can also mix Beauvaria bassiana 1.15% with farmyard manure and apply it to the soil. If termites are already in the field, use Chlorpyrifos 20% EC with irrigation water. Keep the field clean and avoid leaving crop residues. Also, make sure the soil isn't too dry.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My wheat plants are getting weak and dying, what could be the problem?

It might be termites. They attack the roots and make plants easy to pull out. Yellow leaves and stunted growth are signs. To manage them, treat seeds with Chlorpyriphos or thiomethoxam. Use Beauveria bassiana in the soil. Keep fields clean and avoid too much organic matter. Apply Chlorpyrifos with water if needed. Stay alert, especially in October and February.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My paddy plants are getting damaged, and the grains are not filling properly. What could be the problem?

It sounds like you might have Gundhi Bug, also called Naavaai Poochi. These bugs are greenish-brown and can be found on the rice plants. They suck the sap from the grains, making them shriveled and discolored. This happens mostly during the milky stage of the crop. If you see a lot of these bugs, you might need to spray insecticides like Malathion or Imidacloprid. You can also try using neem extract. Make sure to wear protective gear when applying chemicals. Always follow the dosage instructions carefully.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My paddy fields are getting attacked by some bugs that make the grains look bad, what can I do?

The bugs you're seeing are called Gundhi Bugs. They feed on rice grains and make them shriveled and discolored. You can use insecticides like Malathion, Imidacloprid, or Dinotefuran. Apply them in the early morning or evening. Also, try using neem seed extract. Keep your field clean and remove weeds. Wear gloves and a mask when spraying. Don't spray during wind or when flowers are open.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How can I tell if my paddy has Gundhi Bug, and what can I do if it's there?

If your paddy has Gundhi Bug, you might see yellowish or shriveled grains, and the bugs might smell bad when you touch them. They are greenish-brown and about 15-18 mm long. If you find more than 5 bugs per 100 ear heads during flowering or 16 bugs after the milky stage, it's time to act. You can use insecticides like Malathion or Imidacloprid, or try neem extract. Always wear gloves and a mask when spraying. Don't spray during wind or when flowers are open. Keep pesticides in safe places and wash after use.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My paddy plants are getting damaged, and I think it's the Gundhi Bug. What should I do?

The Gundhi Bug attacks paddy during the milky to dough stages. It causes chaffy or shriveled grains and reduces yield. If you find more than 5 bugs per 100 ear heads at flowering or 16 at the milky stage, you need to act. Use insecticides like Malathion 50% EC or Imidacloprid + Lambda-cyhalothrin. Apply them in the early morning or evening. You can also try neem seed extract. Wear protective gear when applying chemicals. Don't spray during flowering or when beneficial insects are around.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My paddy fields are getting damaged by some bugs. What can I do?

The bugs are called Gundhi Bugs. They suck sap from the rice grains, making them shriveled and lighter. You can use insecticides like Malathion or Imidacloprid. Apply them in the early morning or late evening. You can also use neem extract. Wear gloves and a mask when spraying. Keep your fields clean and remove weeds. Don't spray during flowering or when it's windy.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Is it okay to use Nirog on my wheat seeds to keep them healthy?

Yes, you can use Nirog on your wheat seeds. It's a bio-fungicide with Trichoderma viride. For loose smut, mix 4 grams of it per kg of seeds. Make sure the seeds are coated well and dry them in the shade before sowing. Always check the label to make sure it's registered and buy from a trusted dealer.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Is Nirog a good option for treating my wheat seeds?

Yes, Nirog is a biological fungicide that contains Trichoderma viride 1.5% WP. It helps protect wheat seeds from diseases like loose smut and seedling blights. But make sure to check the label for active ingredients and follow the dosage instructions. Always buy genuine products from authorized dealers and check the manufacturing date.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
I heard about a product called Nirog for treating my wheat seeds. Is it safe to use?

Yes, you can use Nirog for treating wheat seeds, but it's a bio-fungicide with Trichoderma viride 1.5% WP. For loose smut, use 4 grams per kg of seeds. Mix it well, shade dry, and then sow. Always check the label for active ingredients and make sure it's registered. Buy from authorized dealers and check the batch number.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Can I use Nirog to treat my wheat seeds?

Yes, you can treat wheat seeds with Nirog. It's a bio-fungicide with Trichoderma viride. For loose smut, use 4 grams per kg of seeds. Mix well, dry in shade, and sow. Also, check the label to make sure it's registered. Buy from authorized dealers and use the right dose.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Is Nirog a good option to use on my wheat seeds?

Nirog is a bio-fungicide that contains Trichoderma viride 1.5% WP. It can be used for treating wheat seeds, but you need to make sure it's registered by the Central Insecticides Board. Always check the label for the active ingredients and follow the recommended dosage. If you're not sure, ask your local dealer for help.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My paddy field has a lot of wild onion, what can I do about it?

You can control wild onion in your paddy by using herbicides. Apply Pretilachlor 50% EC at 600 ml per acre or Pyrazosulfuron ethyl 10% WP at 60 gm per acre within 3 days after transplanting. For later control, use Bispyribac sodium 10% SC at 100 ml per acre at 20-25 days after transplanting. Also, hand weeding at 20-25 and 40-45 days after transplanting works well. Keep 2-3 inches of water in the field when applying the chemicals.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Kya meri chawal ki seedhi mein kuch nahi karna chahiye agar kuch khurang jaise chiz nikal rahi hai?

Agar chawal ki seedhi mein khurang jaise chiz nikal rahi hai, toh usko control karne ke liye pre-emergence herbicides jaise Pretilachlor 50% EC ya Pyrazosulfuron ethyl 10% WP ka upyog karein. 3 din ke andar paribandhi karte samay iska upyog karein. Agar khurang badh gaya, toh Bispyribac sodium 10% SC ka upyog 20-25 din baad karein. Hathi se khurang hatane ka bhi upyog karein. Har baar 2-3 inch pani rakhna chahiye.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My paddy field has a lot of wild onions, what can I do to get rid of them?

To control wild onions in your paddy, you can use herbicides like Pretilachlor 50% EC or Pyrazosulfuron ethyl 10% WP right after transplanting. You can also apply Bispyribac sodium 10% SC later. Hand weeding at 20-25 and 40-45 days after transplanting works too. Keep the water level at 2-3 inches when applying chemicals.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My paddy field has wild onions, how do I get rid of them?

You can control wild onions in paddy by using herbicides. Apply Pretilachlor 50% EC at 600 ml per acre or Pyrazosulfuron ethyl 10% WP at 60 grams per acre within 3 days of transplanting. For later stages, use Bispyribac sodium 10% SC at 100 ml per acre at 20-25 days after transplanting. Also, hand weed the field at 20-25 and 40-45 days. Keep 2-3 inches of water in the field when applying chemicals.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Kya paddy ke khete mein mathia ko khatam karne ki koi aasani wali aam tarika hai?

Haan, mathia ko khatam karne ke liye aap pre-emergence herbicides ka istemal karein. 3 din baad transplant karne ke baad Pretilachlor 50% EC 600 ml/acre ya Pyrazosulfuron ethyl 10% WP 60 gm/acre ka istemal karein. Agar mathia daud raha hai to Bispyribac sodium 10% SC 100 ml/acre 20-25 din baad istemal karein. Hath se khatme bhi karna hota hai 20-25 aur 40-45 din baad. Herbiside ka istemal karne time 2-3 inch pani rakhna hota hai.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My wheat plants are getting some kind of bug that's making the leaves look weird, what could it be?

It might not be whitefly. The problem could be thrips. They are small, brown or black insects that feed on the stem and sheath of the flag leaf. They can make the plant look silver. You can use blue pan traps to check for them. You can paint empty tins blue or use grease, Vaseline, or castor oil on them. Check the traps daily. If you see insects, bring a sample to the nearest KVK for help.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
I'm seeing some small bugs on my wheat plants, what could they be and how do I get rid of them?

Those bugs might be thrips, not whiteflies. They're tiny, brown or black, and move fast. They damage the stem and the sheath of the flag leaf, and can make the plant look silver. To check, use blue sticky traps or paint a tin blue and put it above the crop. Watch the traps daily. If you see bugs, bring a sample to the nearest KVK for help. Don't use chemicals unless you know exactly what you're dealing with.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
I've noticed some tiny bugs on my wheat plants, what should I do?

Those tiny bugs might be thrips, not whiteflies. They're small, brown or black, and move fast. They feed on the stem and sheath of the flag leaf, and can make the plant look silver. To check, use blue pan traps or sticky traps. You can paint tins blue or use Vaseline on them. Check the traps daily. If you see insects, take a sample to the nearest KVK for help identifying the pest and getting the right control advice.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My wheat plants are getting damaged, and I think it's something small. What could it be?

It might not be whitefly. The problem could be thrips. They are tiny, brown or black bugs that feed on the stem and leaves. They can make the plant look silver. To check, use blue traps or sticky traps. If you see bugs, take a sample to the KVK for help.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
I noticed small brown bugs on my wheat plants, what are they and how do I deal with them?

Those small brown bugs are thrips. They can damage the wheat by feeding on the stem and leaves, causing a silver color on the plant. To check for thrips, use blue pan traps or sticky traps. You can paint empty tins blue or use grease on them. Count the bugs daily and decide on the next step. If you're not sure, take a sample to the nearest KVK for help.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My stored wheat is getting attacked by little red bugs, what can I do?

Those red bugs are called rust-red flour beetles. They damage stored wheat by eating it and leaving a musty smell. To stop them, keep your storage area clean and dry. Dry the wheat properly before storing and use sealed containers. For small amounts, expose the wheat to sunlight for a few hours. If it's badly infested, you can use fumigation with Aluminum Phosphide, but it needs to be done carefully. Always follow safety steps and make sure the area is well-ventilated before using the grain again.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
What can I do if my stored wheat has got bugs in it?

If your stored wheat has bugs, you can expose it to direct sunlight for 4-5 hours to kill the beetles. Sieve the grain to remove the bugs and larvae. Store the wheat in airtight containers to prevent re-infestation. For heavy infestations, use Aluminum Phosphide fumigation as recommended. Make sure to follow safety precautions and aerate the grain properly before using it. Keep storage areas clean and dry to avoid future problems.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My wheat is getting damaged after I store it, what can I do?

To stop your stored wheat from getting damaged, keep the storage area clean and free of old grains. Dry the wheat properly before storing so the moisture is below 12%. Store it in airtight containers or sealed bins. If the wheat is already infested, expose it to sunlight for 4-5 hours to kill the beetles. You can also use diatomaceous earth or neem seed powder to protect it. For serious infestations, fumigation with Aluminum Phosphide is effective but needs to be done carefully. Always follow safety rules and make sure the storage is sealed properly. Let the grain aerate for at least 48 hours after fumigation before using it.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
What can I do to get rid of flour beetles in my stored wheat?

To get rid of flour beetles in stored wheat, you can expose the grain to direct sunlight for 4-5 hours, which kills all stages of the beetle. For small amounts, sieve the grain to remove beetles and larvae. Store wheat in airtight containers or sealed metallic bins. If the infestation is heavy, use Aluminum Phosphide 56% for fumigation. Follow the recommended dosage and make sure the storage area is completely sealed. After fumigation, aerate the area for 48-72 hours before handling the grain. You can also mix diatomaceous earth or neem seed kernel powder with the grain for added protection.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My stored wheat has a bad smell and tiny bugs in it, what can I do?

The bad smell and tiny bugs in your stored wheat are signs of a rust-red flour beetle infestation. These pests are small, reddish-brown, and found in flour and broken grains. To control them, expose the wheat to direct sunlight for 4-5 hours, which kills all life stages of the beetle. Sieve the grain to remove the bugs. Store the wheat in airtight containers and keep the storage area cool. For heavy infestations, fumigation with Aluminum Phosphide is effective, but it should be done by trained personnel. Make sure to follow all safety precautions and aerate the grain properly before using it.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My Berseem crop is getting damaged by some caterpillars, what can I do?

Some caterpillars like Bihar hairy caterpillar and gram caterpillar can harm your Berseem crop. These pests eat the plants and can destroy them completely. To stop them, remove weeds near your fields before planting. Also, avoid growing Berseem near crops like tomatoes or sunflowers, as these can attract the pests. If needed, spray Spinosad 45% SC at 60-62 ml per acre when the pests are a problem.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
What can I do to stop the caterpillars from eating my berseem plants?

To stop the caterpillars from damaging your berseem plants, you need to get rid of weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak, and wild castor near your fields before planting. These weeds are where the pests live and then move to your berseem. Also, keep your berseem away from crops like tomato, gram, and sunflower. If needed, spray Spinosad 45% SC at 60-62ml per acre when the pests are a problem.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
The leaves of my berseem crop are getting eaten up, what's causing this and how can I fix it?

The damage to your berseem crop is likely caused by the Bihar hairy caterpillar or gram caterpillar. These pests attack the crop in two phases, one in September-October and again in March-April. To prevent this, remove weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak, and wild castor near your fields before sowing. Also, avoid growing berseem near tomato, gram, or other crops that attract the pest. If needed, spray Spinosad 45% SC at 60-62ml per acre when the pest is at an economic threshold level.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My berseem plants are getting eaten up, what's causing it and how can I stop it?

The damage to your berseem plants is likely caused by Bihar hairy caterpillar or gram caterpillar. These pests attack the crop at different times of the year. To prevent this, remove weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak, and wild castor near your fields before sowing. Also, avoid growing berseem near tomato, gram, or sunflower. If needed, spray Spinosad 45% SC at 60-62 ml per acre when the pest is present.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My Berseem crop is getting eaten up by small worms, what can I do?

The small worms are likely Bihar hairy caterpillars or gram caterpillars. These pests attack Berseem crops, especially in September-October and March-April. To control them, remove weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak, and wild castor near your fields before sowing. Also, avoid growing Berseem near tomato, gram, or sunflower. If needed, spray Spinosad 45% SC at 60-62ml per acre when the pests are present.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My sugarcane plants are getting damaged, and I think it's because of borers. What can I do?

Borers can really hurt your sugarcane. They cause dead hearts, bunchy tops, and tunnels inside the stems. To control early shoot borer, you can use Chlorpyrifos 20% EC. Apply 2.5 liters per hectare as a soil drench. You can also use Carbofuran 3% CG or Phorate 10% CG. Wear protective gear when handling chemicals. Release Trichogramma chilonis wasps and Cotesia flavipes to help control the pests. Remove dead hearts and infested parts. Check your fields regularly and act when 5-10% of plants show symptoms.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My sugarcane plants are getting damaged by borers, what can I do?

Borers can really hurt your sugarcane crop. There are different types, like the early shoot borer and the internode borer. They cause things like dead hearts and weak stems. To control them, you can use Chlorpyrifos 20% EC at 2.5 liters per hectare as a soil drench. You can also use Carbofuran 3% CG or Phorate 10% CG. Always wear protection when handling these chemicals. You can also use natural enemies like Trichogramma chilonis and Cotesia flavipes. Keep an eye on your plants and act when you see 5-10% of them showing signs of damage.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My sugarcane plants have some dead hearts, what could be causing it?

Dead hearts in sugarcane are often caused by borer infestations. Early shoot borers and top borers can cause this symptom. The larvae bore into the center of the plant, leading to wilting and drying of the central leaf. It's important to check for other signs like bunchy tops or tunnels inside the cane. You can use Chlorpyrifos 20% EC or other granular insecticides as recommended. Also, consider biological control like Trichogramma chilonis. Keep an eye on the plants and take action early.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My sugarcane plants are getting damaged, and some have dead hearts. What can I do?

To control borer infestation in sugarcane, you can use Chlorpyrifos 20% EC at 2.5 liters per hectare as a soil drench. You can also use Carbofuran 3% CG or Phorate 10% CG. Wear protective gear when applying chemicals. Release Trichogramma chilonis and Cotesia flavipes for natural control. Remove dead hearts and infested shoots. Monitor your field regularly and act when 5-10% of plants show symptoms.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My sugarcane plants are getting eaten from the inside, what can I do?

The problem is likely caused by borers, which tunnel inside the cane and damage the stems. To control them, you can use Chlorpyrifos 20% EC as a soil drench at 2.5 liters per hectare during the early growth stage. You can also use Carbofuran 3% CG or Phorate 10% CG as soil treatments. Always wear protective gear when applying these chemicals. Regularly check your fields and remove any infested shoots. Using biological controls like Trichogramma chilonis can also help manage the pests.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Why are my sugarcane plants not producing enough shoots this season?

Your sugarcane might not be producing enough shoots because of short days, not enough water during the tillering stage, or a lack of nutrients like phosphorus, copper, or zinc. Poor irrigation, low phosphorus, or copper deficiency can also cause yellowing or discoloration of leaves and weak growth. Zinc deficiency can lead to yellow leaf blades and short stalks. To help, make sure you water enough during key growth stages, use 2% DAP foliar spray twice, and apply the right fertilizers. Test your soil before planting and use certified seeds to avoid diseases.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Why isn't my sugarcane making enough shoots this year?

Your sugarcane might not be making enough shoots because of short winter days, not enough water during the tillering stage, or a lack of phosphorus, copper, or zinc in the soil. Poor irrigation, low sunlight, and diseases like ratoon stunting can also cause this. To fix it, make sure to water properly, especially during key growth stages. You can spray 2% DAP twice, a fortnight apart, and add zinc sulphate if needed. Also, use healthy setts and remove any infected plants.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Why is my sugarcane not growing many side shoots this year?

Your sugarcane might not be producing many side shoots because of short days, not enough water during the tillering stage, or a lack of phosphorus, copper, or zinc. Also, ratoon stunting disease can cause this. Make sure to water properly, use the right fertilizers, and check for disease. A soil test can help find what's missing.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Why are my sugarcane plants not making enough side shoots?

Low tillering in sugarcane can happen for many reasons. Short days can make plants thin and weak. Not enough water during this time also stops tillers from forming. If the soil is low in phosphorus, the plants get weak and don’t grow well. Copper or zinc deficiencies can cause leaf problems and fewer side shoots. Ratoon stunting disease can also stop tillering. To help, water properly, use 2% DAP spray, add zinc if needed, and use healthy seeds. Test your soil first and add nutrients it lacks.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
What can I do to fix poor sugarcane tillering?

To fix poor sugarcane tillering, make sure to water the plants properly during the tillering stage. Apply 2% DAP foliar spray twice, with a fortnight between each. Use the right amount of fertilizers as recommended. If the soil is low in zinc, add 15 kg zinc sulphate per acre before the last ploughing. Also, use setts from disease-free fields and remove any infected plants. Check the soil before sowing and add the nutrients the soil lacks.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How often should I water my cotton plants and when is the best time to do it?

You should water your cotton plants 4 to 6 times depending on the rain. The first watering should be 4 to 6 weeks after planting, then every two or three weeks after that. If you have light soil or planted on ridges, you might need to water earlier. Make sure not to let the plants get too dry during flowering and fruiting, or they might drop flowers and bolls. Avoid watering in the middle of the day—early morning or evening is better. Drain any standing water if it happens. In the end, water by the end of September to help the bolls open. Don't overwater during boll opening, or it might hurt the quality of the cotton.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
When should I water my cotton crop and how often?

You should water your cotton crop 4 to 6 times depending on the rain. The first watering should be 4 to 6 weeks after sowing, and then every two to three weeks. If you're growing cotton on ridges, you might need to water a bit earlier. Make sure not to let the crop get too dry during flowering and fruiting, or it might lose a lot of flowers and bolls. Watering in the early morning or evening is best, and avoid watering during hot afternoons. Also, don't overwater, as cotton doesn't like standing water. The last watering should be by the end of September to help the bolls open. For exact timing, check your soil and weather.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How often should I water my cotton plants during the early stages?

During the early stages, you should water your cotton plants 4 to 6 times depending on the rain. The first watering should be 4 to 6 weeks after sowing. Make sure the water doesn't stay too long, as cotton doesn't like standing water. Keep the soil moist but not soaked. If the soil is light or you planted on ridges, you might need to water a bit earlier if needed. Don't forget to drain any water that builds up. Proper watering helps the plants grow strong and avoid problems later.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How often should I water my cotton plants?

You should water your cotton plants 4 to 6 times depending on the rain. The first water should be given 4 to 6 weeks after planting. Then water every 2 to 3 weeks. On light soil or if you plant on ridges, you may need to water earlier. Water in the furrows to save water. If the water isn’t good, use canal water first and then tube well water in alternate furrows. Don’t let the plants get too dry during flowering and boll formation, or they’ll drop flowers and bolls. Avoid waterlogging, especially in the early stages. Drain any standing water. Water the last time by the end of September. For drip irrigation, water every 7 days with proper setup. Adjust time if your drippers give different flow. Don’t water in the hot afternoon. Water in the morning or evening. If it rains, skip the planned water. Good irrigation can boost yield by 30-40%.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How do I make sure I'm watering my cotton crop the right way?

Water your cotton crop at the right times to get the best results. Give the first irrigation 4 to 6 weeks after sowing, and then every two or three weeks. If you have light soil or planted on ridges, you might need to water earlier. Use drip irrigation for Bt cotton hybrids, with 7-day intervals. Adjust the time based on the dripper rate. Don't overwater, especially during flowering and fruiting, or you'll lose flowers and bolls. Drain any standing water. Water in the early morning or evening, not in the heat of the day. Avoid waterlogging to prevent root rot. If it rains, skip the scheduled irrigation. Proper watering can boost your yield by 30-40%.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How do I make a waste decomposer for my wheat field?

To make a waste decomposer, mix 2 kg of jaggery in 200 liters of water in a plastic drum. Add one bottle of waste decomposer, stir with a wooden stick, and cover it with paper or cardboard. Stir once or twice a day. After five days, the solution will turn creamy and be ready to use. You can reuse the solution by adding 20 liters of it to a new drum with 2 kg of jaggery and 200 liters of water. This helps improve soil health and can be used on your wheat field.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
What is Sagarika, and can it be used for my wheat crop?

Sagarika is a seaweed-based biostimulant made from red and brown algae. It helps improve wheat growth, root development, and stress resistance. It comes in liquid and granule forms and should be used along with regular fertilizers, not as a replacement. It helps with nutrient efficiency and makes plants stronger. You can use Sagarika Z++ granules at 10 kg per acre at sowing and again 15–20 days later. The liquid form is sprayed on leaves at 500 ml per acre. It’s good for soil health and plant vigor.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Is there any good biostimulant I can use for my wheat crop?

Yes, Sagarika is a seaweed-based biostimulant that helps improve wheat growth and resilience. It comes in liquid and granular forms and supports better nutrient use and plant health. It’s not a substitute for regular fertilizers but works alongside them. It also helps the crop handle stress from drought or heat. You can use Sagarika Z++ Granules at 10 kg per acre at sowing and again 15–20 days later. Sagarika Liquid is sprayed on leaves at 500 ml per acre. It contains seaweed extract, bio-potash, and nutrients that boost plant vigor and yield.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
What is Sagarika and how does it help my wheat crop?

Sagarika is a seaweed-based biostimulant made from red and brown algae. It helps improve plant growth, root development, and stress tolerance in wheat. It also boosts soil health and makes plants stronger. It comes in liquid and granular forms. The granules have bio-potash, zinc, boron, and other nutrients. You can apply it at sowing and again 15-20 days later. It's not a replacement for regular fertilizer but works along with it. It helps plants handle drought, heat, and salinity better, which can lead to better yields and healthier crops.

My maize plants are looking sick, and the leaves are turning yellow. What's going on?

My maize plants are looking yellow, what could be the problem and how do I fix it?

Your maize plants turning yellow might be due to a lack of nitrogen or zinc. For nitrogen deficiency, older leaves turn yellow starting from the tip. Apply 50 kg nitrogen per acre for most varieties, which is about 110 kg urea. For some varieties, use 35 kg nitrogen per acre. Split the nitrogen into three parts—use one-third at sowing, one-third at the knee-high stage, and the rest before tasseling. Why can't I use zinc and phosphorus in dry fields before planting paddy?

Zinc and phosphorus don't work well in dry soil before planting paddy. The plants can't take them in properly when the soil is dry. It's better to add them when the field is wet, like during the last land preparation. This helps the nutrients stay available for the plants. If you use them in dry soil, they might get stuck and not help the crop, which wastes money and nutrients.

My paddy seedlings got hit by hail, and now they look all torn up. What can I do?

After a hailstorm, check your paddy seedlings for torn leaves, broken stems, or bruised areas. These injuries can let in fungus or bacteria. Drain any water from the nursery beds right away and remove the worst damaged plants. Gently straighten the ones that are bent. You can also spray a fungicide like Carbendazim or Mancozeb to protect the seedlings from infection. If the problem is bad, you might need to start over with new seedlings.

I'm growing wheat in Uttar Pradesh, and I want to know how to apply fertilizers properly?

For wheat in Uttar Pradesh, apply 60 kg nitrogen per acre as 130 kg urea, 24 kg phosphorus as 150 kg SSP, and 16 kg potassium as 27 kg MOP. At sowing, mix 50% nitrogen, all phosphorus, and all potassium into the soil. Then, apply the rest of the nitrogen at first irrigation, around 21-25 days after sowing. If your plants look yellow between the veins, add 10 kg zinc sulphate with the base dose. For high-yield varieties, you might need more nitrogen. Always mix fertilizers well and avoid applying them on dry soil.

My pea plants are getting yellow leaves at the bottom, what's going on?

The yellowing of lower leaves in your pea plants is a sign of wilt disease. This happens because of root rot caused by fungi like Fusariumoxysporum and Rhizoctonia solani. The leaves turn yellow, and the plants may get stunted. The stems might look swollen and brittle near the soil. To manage this, treat your seeds with Carbendazim 50% WP or Captan before sowing. Also, use the right amount of fertilizer based on soil testing. You can add 8 tonnes of farmyard manure and 20 kg of nitrogen and 25 kg of phosphorus per acre. If you're planting peas for the first time in an area, treat the seeds with Rhizobiumleguminosarum to help them grow better.

They also leave a sticky coating that can lead to mold. To control them, you can use insecticides like Malathion or Thiamethoxam. Make sure to spray when the aphid count gets high, and avoid using too much nitrogen in your fertilizer. Also, consider planting resistant wheat varieties.

You can also spray Malathion or Thiamethoxam when there are 10-15 aphids per earhead. Spray in the morning or evening, and cover both sides of the leaves. What is the Fasal Bima Yojana all about?

My paddy field has salty soil, what can I do to fix it?

To fix salty soil in your paddy field, add organic matter like paddy or wheat straw. Mix it into the soil up to 15–20 cm deep in April or May. Water it 2–3 times to help break down the residues and wash away salts. Also, plow deep to improve drainage and use green manure like dhaincha before planting. If the soil is very salty, use gypsum as recommended by a soil test. Keep adding organic matter over the years and choose salt-tolerant paddy varieties.

I think I heard about a fertilizer called 20:20:13 for my maize. Is that right, and can I spray it on the leaves?

The fertilizer 20:20:13 doesn't exist. You might be thinking of 20:20:0:13, which gives nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur but no potassium. This fertilizer is meant for soil application, not spraying on leaves. You should apply it during sowing by broadcasting. Don't mix it with standing crops. If your crop shows sulfur deficiency, you can use magnesium sulfate at 1% (10 grams in 1 liter water). Always do a soil test before applying any fertilizer.

Isko 100 kg ke rate se kholke dene se 20 kg nitrojen, 20 kg fosfor aur 13 kg sulphur pata chalta hai. Isme potash nahi hota. Is fertilizer ko kheer mein utha ke dene ka sahi tarika hai. Iska istemal foliar spray ke roop mein nahi kiya jana chahiye. Agar kheer me sulphur ke kame ki tanda hai to 1% magnesium sulphate (10 gm per liter pani) ka istemal kiya jaye. What's the right amount of fertilizer to use for my mustard crop?

You should use about 1.2 tonnes of well-decomposed FYM per acre before sowing. At sowing time, apply 18 kg urea, 101 kg SSP, and 27 kg MOP per acre. SSP helps with root growth, while urea and MOP give nitrogen and potash. For top dressing, use 35 kg urea at the branching stage and 18 kg urea at the flowering stage. Also, water the crop twice during those stages. If your soil is low in sulfur, add 18 kg per acre.

What's the right amount of fertilizer to use when planting sugarcane?

At planting time, sugarcane needs a good mix of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. A general recommendation is 60 kg of nitrogen, which is about 130 kg of urea, for planting. If the soil is low in phosphorus and potassium, add 12 kg of phosphorus and 30 kg of potassium per acre. Apply all the phosphorus and potassium right at planting. For ratoon crops, give one-third of the nitrogen in February. If the soil is dry, apply half the nitrogen at planting and the rest when it rains. Fertilizers should go in the furrow, mixed with the soil, and kept 5-7 cm away from the sugarcane sets to avoid burning. You can also add 8 tonnes of farmyard manure per acre before planting.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How much fertilizer should I use when planting sugarcane?

At planting time, sugarcane needs a balanced mix of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. For the main crop, use about 60 kg of nitrogen, which is 130 kg of urea. For ratoon crops, use 90 kg of nitrogen, or 195 kg of urea. If the soil is low in phosphorus and potassium, add 12 kg of phosphorus (75 kg of SSP) and 30 kg of potassium (50 kg of MOP) per acre. Apply all the phosphorus and potassium when you plant. For ratoon crops, add one-third of the nitrogen in February. If the soil is dry, apply half the nitrogen at planting. Apply fertilizers in the furrow, mix them into the soil, and cover before planting the sugarcane sets. Also, add 8 tonnes of farmyard manure per acre 15 days before planting.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How should I apply fertiliser when planting sugarcane?

Apply all the phosphorus and potash in the furrow at planting time. For nitrogen, use 130 kg of urea per acre at planting and 195 kg for ratoon crops. If the soil is low in phosphorus and potassium, add 75 kg of SSP and 50 kg of MOP per acre. Mix the fertiliser into the soil and cover it before placing the setts. Keep the fertiliser 5-7 cm away from the setts to avoid burning. You can also add 8 tonnes of FYM or press-mud per acre 15 days before planting. If you're using bio-fertilisers like Azotobacter, apply 4 kg per acre in the furrows at planting.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
My sugarcane plants are not growing strong, what could be the problem?

If your sugarcane plants are not growing strong, it could be due to improper fertiliser application. At planting, make sure to apply 60 kg of nitrogen (130 kg of urea) and 12 kg of phosphorus (75 kg of SSP) along with 30 kg of potassium (50 kg of MOP) per acre. Apply all the phosphorus and potassium at planting, and split the nitrogen into parts. If the soil is dry, apply half the nitrogen at planting and the rest when rains come. Also, mix 8 tonnes of FYM or press-mud into the soil 15 days before planting. Using bio-fertilisers like Azotobacter or consortium can also help improve growth.

Place fertiliser 5-7 cm away from the setts to avoid burning. If you have organic matter like FYM, use 8 tonnes per acre 15 days before planting. Don't forget to test your soil before applying fertiliser.

My wheat plants are falling over, will that affect how much grain I get?

Yes, if your wheat plants are falling over, it can definitely hurt your yield. When the plants bend or fall, they get less sunlight and don't make as much food. This makes the grains not fill properly. In India, lodging can cut wheat yields by 12 to 66 percent. If it happens when the grains are forming, the loss can be as high as 80 percent. It also makes harvesting harder and can lead to more disease.

My cotton has dark spots on the lint, what's causing that and how can I stop it?

The dark spots on your cotton lint are caused by honeydew from pests like aphids, whiteflies, and mealybugs. This sticky substance helps mold grow, which makes the lint look black. To stop this, spray Imidacloprid or Thiamethoxam for aphids, Dimethoate or Neem-based sprays for whiteflies, and Thiodicarb or Quinalphos for mealybugs. Keep your field clean, avoid too much nitrogen, and harvest when the cotton is dry. Always wear protection when using sprays.

My cotton is getting dark, what's causing that and how can I stop it?

The darkening of your cotton is likely due to honeydew from pests like aphids, whiteflies, and mealybugs. This sticky substance attracts sooty mold, which turns the lint black. It can also happen from bacterial or fungal infections, especially in wet weather. To stop it, keep the field clean, avoid too much nitrogen, and harvest when the cotton is dry. Spray Imidacloprid or Thiamethoxam for aphids, and Dimethoate or Neem-based insecticides for whiteflies. For mealybugs, use Thiodicarb or Quinalphos. Always wear protection when spraying and follow the instructions on the label.

My pea plants are not growing well, and the roots look bad. What's going on?

The problem might be root-knot nematodes. They attack the roots and cause stunting, yellowing leaves, and root galls. The roots may look brown and water-soaked. To control this, plough deeply in summer, use solarization with plastic sheets, and plant resistant varieties. Don't sow late, and check the soil before planting. Keep the field clean and avoid spreading infected soil.

How do I manage nutrients and pests in my cotton field without using too much chemicals?

Use urea and SSP for nitrogen and phosphorus, and add zinc sulphate. You can also spray urea or potassium nitrate on the leaves for extra nutrients. To keep the soil pH right for paddy, you should aim for a range of 5.5 to 7.0. In Punjab, especially in areas like Muktsar, applying gypsum at 200-400 kg per acre based on soil test results helps fix alkaline soil. Spread the gypsum and mix it into the soil before planting. Adding farmyard manure and green manure crops like dhaincha also helps. Keep water at 5-7 cm depth in the field. Test your soil every 2-3 years and avoid using too much urea. These steps help your paddy grow better and give higher yields.

How do I fix the pH in my paddy field if it's too alkaline?

If your paddy field is too alkaline, you can use gypsum to help fix the problem. Apply 200-400 kg of gypsum per acre based on your soil test. Gypsum replaces sodium in the soil with calcium, which helps lower the pH. Spread the gypsum evenly and mix it into the soil before transplanting paddy. Adding farmyard manure and growing green manure crops like dhaincha can also help balance the pH. Keep the field flooded with 5-7 cm of water to further reduce alkalinity. Make sure to test your soil every 2-3 years to keep the pH in the right range.

My paddy field is losing a lot of water, what can I do?

Water is moving down too fast in your paddy field, which wastes water and takes away nutrients. To fix this, make sure the soil is properly plowed and rolled with a heavy roller. You can also add clay or farmyard manure to the soil. Puddling the field after plowing helps create a layer that holds water better. Make sure the field is level and the bunds are in good shape. These steps will help keep water in the root zone longer.

My sugarcane plants are getting red leaves and some look burnt, what can I do?

Mites can cause red and burnt-looking leaves on sugarcane. They like hot, dry weather and hide under webs on the bottom of leaves. My wheat plants have brown spots on the leaves, what can I do?

The brown spots on your wheat leaves are likely from a fungal disease called brown spot. It's common in areas like Pilibhit, especially when it's humid and warm. The spots start small and get bigger, turning the leaves yellow and making them look scorched. To manage it, plant resistant varieties like HD-2967 or PBW-550. Also, rotate crops with things like pulses or mustard. Make sure to treat seeds with Carboxin and Thiram before planting. If the problem gets bad, you can spray fungicides like Thiophanate-methyl or Mancozeb. Apply the spray when you first see the spots, and again after 15-20 days if needed. Always wear protective gear while spraying.

How does that machine work that plants the rice seedlings in Punjab?

The machine is called a paddy transplanter. It picks seedlings from special trays and plants them in the field. It works with a tractor and does all the work in one go. It plants 2 to 3 seedlings at a time and keeps them spaced evenly. The machine has parts that help it plant at the right depth. It can cover a lot of land quickly, which saves a lot of work. There are different types of these machines, like walk-behind or four-wheel models. The spacing between the rows is about 30 cm, and the plants are spaced 14 cm apart. This helps the rice grow better and gives a better harvest.

What nutrients do paddy plants take in from the air?

Paddy plants take in some nutrients from the air. They get carbon from carbon dioxide, oxygen through their leaves, and hydrogen from water in the air and soil. My maize plants are not growing well, and the new leaves look all twisted. What could be the problem?

It sounds like your maize might be suffering from calcium deficiency. This happens in soils that don’t have enough calcium, are too acidic, or have poor root growth. The symptoms include twisted or curled leaves, weak stems, and poor cob development. To fix this, you can add lime to the soil before planting, keep the soil moist but not waterlogged, and add compost or manure. You can also spray a 2% calcium sulphate solution on the leaves twice. What potato varieties should I grow, when should I plant them, and how much fertilizer do I need?

Pyrilla is a sucking pest that affects maize, especially in Punjab during the Kharif season. It often comes from sugarcane fields. To control it, keep your field clean by removing crop residues. Check leaves regularly for nymphs and adults. Use chemical pesticides only if needed. Early action is important to avoid losses.

I'm planning to sow wheat, but I'm not sure when to start. What's the best time to plant in Haryana?

In Haryana, wheat should be sown from the last week of October to the first week of November if you have limited irrigation. For irrigated areas, the best time is between October 25th and November 15th. The soil temperature should be between 20-25°C for good germination. Sowing too early or too late can hurt your crop. Make sure the soil has enough moisture but isn't too wet or dry. Also, use good quality seeds and prepare the field properly with ploughing and leveling.

When should I plant my wheat in Haryana, and what if I plant too late?

In Haryana, wheat should be planted from late October to early November if you don’t have much irrigation. If you have irrigation, plant between October 25th and November 15th. If you plant too late, like after mid-December, the plants won’t tiller well and might not produce as much. The soil temperature should be between 20-25°C for good germination. If it’s too hot, seeds won’t grow well. If you plant too late, you might need to use more seeds to get a good crop.

If it’s too hot or too cold, the seeds might not grow well.

What should I do if termites are damaging my wheat seeds after sowing?

It often happens when the weather is wet and the soil is waterlogged. To manage it, make sure your field drains well, use clean seeds, and apply fungicides like Metalaxyl or Thiram. What can I do if my maize plants have white leaves?

Apply well-decomposed farmyard manure and ensure proper drainage. If symptoms appear, spray Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP or Fosetyl-Al 80% WP. Also, apply zinc sulphate to prevent deficiency. My maize plants are not growing well, and the leaves are turning pink. What could be the problem?

It sounds like your maize might be suffering from phosphorus deficiency. Phosphorus is important for plant growth, and a lack of it can cause stunted growth and pinkish color on older leaves. You should get your soil tested to check the phosphorus levels. If it's low, you can apply DAP or SSP according to the variety you're growing. For example, some varieties need 24kg of phosphorus as DAP per acre. If the problem is already showing, a foliar spray of 2% DAP might help. Make sure the soil pH is between 6.5 and 7.5 for best results.

My maize plants are growing slowly and the older leaves are turning pink, what can I do?

Phosphorus works best in soil with a pH of 6.5 to 7.5. If the soil is too alkaline, you may need to add gypsum. You can also apply DAP as a base fertilizer. For example, use 55 kg of DAP per acre for certain varieties. If the problem appears later, a 2% DAP foliar spray can help. Always follow the recommended amounts for your specific crop type.

How do I fix phosphorus deficiency in my maize crop?

To fix phosphorus deficiency in maize, apply the right amount of phosphorus based on the variety you're growing. For irrigated varieties, use 24kg of phosphorus as DAP 55kg per acre. For some other varieties, use 27kg DAP. If the soil is sandy, use 18kg DAP. If the plants show symptoms, spray 2% DAP on the leaves. Also, test the soil to know the right amount of phosphorus needed and use gypsum if the soil is too alkaline.

Can I mix Imidacloprid and SAAF when spraying paddy?

Yes, Imidacloprid and SAAF can be mixed for paddy. They work well together to control pests and diseases. Mix 40-50 ml of Imidacloprid with 400 grams of SAAF in 200 liters of water. Always do a jar test first. Add SAAF first, then Imidacloprid. Don't mix with anything else unless told to. Use the mixture right away and clean your equipment after.

Can I mix Imidacloprid with SAAF for paddy, or will it cause problems?

Yes, you can mix Imidacloprid and SAAF for paddy. They work well together and help control pests and diseases at the same time. To be safe, do a jar test first. Mix a little of each in a jar with water, shake it, and wait 30 minutes. If it stays smooth and doesn’t get warm, it’s okay. If it gets warm or clumps, don’t use it. Always add SAAF first, then Imidacloprid, and use the mix right away. Don’t mix with other chemicals unless told to do so.

Mix a little of each in a jar with water, shake, and check after 30 minutes. If it stays smooth and cool, it's safe. If it gets warm or clumps, don't use it. Always mix the fungicide first, then add the imidacloprid. Use the mix right away and don't store it for more than a few hours.

I want to plant Raya late, but I'm not sure which varieties are good for that. What should I do?

If you're planting Raya late, you should use varieties like PBR 91 or PBR 97. PBR 91 is good for November sowing and gives about 8.1 quintals per acre. PBR 97 matures faster and handles heat better. Don't plant main-season varieties like RLC 3 or PBR 357 in November, as they'll get damaged by aphids. There are also other options like PHR 127, RCH 1, PHR 126, Giriraj, and RLC 3, but these are better for timely sowing.

There are also some late-maturing options like PHR 127 and RCH 1 if you're looking for that.

My citrus trees are getting sick, what can I do?

Citrus crops in Punjab, like Kinnow, Orange, and Lemon, can get sick from diseases like Gummosis, Canker, and others. For Gummosis, spray Fosetyl-Aluminium 80% w/w at 2.5 grams per liter of water on the trunk in April and September. For Canker, use Copper Oxychloride 50% WP at 3 grams per liter of water every 15 days during the rainy season. For Sooty Mold, mix Ziram 27 SC and Ethion 50 EC in water and spray once a month from August to October. Always wear protective gear when applying chemicals and follow safety rules.

My citrus trees in Punjab are looking sick, what could be wrong and how do I fix it?

For gummosis, spray Fosetyl-Aluminium 80% at 2.5 grams per liter of water on the trunk in April and September. For canker, use Copper Oxychloride 50% WP at 3 grams per liter during the rainy season. Keep the orchard clean, avoid waterlogging, and spray fungicides as needed. Always wear protective gear when applying chemicals.

Treat seeds with Carboxin or Thiram. Space rows 20-23 cm apart and plant 5-6 cm deep. Give it 4-5 irrigations, especially during crown root and flowering stages. It needs 20-24 kg nitrogen and 12 kg phosphorus per acre. It makes good chapatis and gives 10-12 quintals per acre. You can get seeds from BHU, UPSSC, KVK, or the district agriculture office.

When should I put lime in my potato field?

My cotton plants are getting damaged, and I think it's a type of weevil. What should I do?

It sounds like you might be dealing with stem or shoot weevils. Stem weevils make small holes in the stems and lay eggs. The grubs damage stems, branches, and roots, causing swelling and wilting. Shoot weevils make galls on the tips of shoots. To control them, plow deeply in summer, keep the field clean, rotate crops, and use trap crops like castor or marigold. You can also apply farmyard manure or neem cake. If you're not sure, take samples to the nearest KVK for help.

My potato plants are getting brown spots on the leaves, what can I do?

The brown spots on your potato leaves are likely from early blight. This disease starts with small brown spots that grow bigger and look like bull's eyes. It also affects the tubers, making them shriveled and dry. To manage it, use disease-free seeds and give balanced fertilizers. Spray 1% urea at 45 days after sowing and repeat every 8-10 days. Mix 9 kg of urea in 300 liters of water and spray it over one acre. Make sure to spray in a crosswise direction for even coverage. You can also apply urea in stages during key growth stages like CRI and tillering. Always test the soil first to know exactly what your crop needs.

How much fertilizer should I use for my wheat crop, and when should I apply it?

For wheat, you should use 120-150 kg of nitrogen, 60 kg of phosphorus, and 40 kg of potassium per hectare. Apply all the phosphorus and potassium at sowing, along with 25% of the nitrogen. Then apply half the remaining nitrogen 20-25 days after sowing, and the last quarter at 40-45 days. Use 130 kg of DAP and 65 kg of muriate of potash at sowing. For the rest, use 100-120 kg of urea at the CRI stage and 65-80 kg at the tillering to jointing stage. Make sure the soil is moist when applying. You can also add 10-12 tonnes of farm yard manure before sowing.

My chilli plants are getting damaged, and I see little worms eating the pods. What's going on?

It sounds like you're dealing with Fruit Borer, not Spotted Bollworm. The caterpillars are brown at first and turn green with dark lines as they grow. They eat the inside of the pods and leave holes. To manage them, use neem cake at transplanting, set up pheromone traps, and release Trichogramma wasps. You can also spray Chlorantraniliprole or Spinosad as needed. Make sure to wear protective gear when applying chemicals.

Set up pheromone traps to monitor the moths. Also, plant marigold as a trap crop. If you need to spray, use Chlorantraniliprole or Spinosad, but follow the safety rules. How much sulphur should I use for my cotton crop?

For hybrid or Bt cotton, you should use 150 kg of SSP per acre. For American cotton, 75 kg of SSP per acre is enough. SSP has 11–12% sulphur, so this gives around 16–18 kg of sulphur for hybrid cotton and 8–9 kg for American cotton. Sulphur helps with growth and prevents diseases like grey mildew and mites. For mites, you can spray 5 grams of water-soluble sulphur in a liter of water. Make sure to follow the local guidelines for best results.

My wheat plants are getting some kind of disease, what can I do?

Wheat can get sick from fungal or bacterial diseases. Some common ones in Haryana are leaf rust, yellow rust, powdery mildew, and karnal bunt. You can use fungicides like Mancozeb 75% WP, Propiconazole 25% EC, or Tebuconazole 25% WG. For example, mix 800 grams of Mancozeb in 200-250 liters of water and spray. For rust, use 200 ml of Propiconazole or 300 grams of Tebuconazole per acre. Sulfur 80% WP works for powdery mildew. Apply at the right time—like when the plants are at the tillering stage or during flowering. You can also use biological options like Trichoderma or Pseudomonas. Plant resistant varieties and keep the field clean. Always wear gloves and a mask when spraying.

Apply 200 ml of Propiconazole or 300 g of Tebuconazole per acre with 200 liters of water. It's best to spray when the disease first appears or during the tillering to boot leaf stage. You might need a second spray 15-20 days later if the problem continues. Also, make sure to use resistant wheat varieties and treat seeds with Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% WS before planting.

My mustard plants are getting damaged by tiny bugs, what can I do?

The tiny bugs are aphids, which suck the sap from your mustard plants. They can make the plants weak, cause leaves to curl, and stop the seeds from developing. To control them, try sowing early, use resistant varieties, and set up yellow sticky traps. If the aphid count gets high, spray insecticides like thiamethoxam or dimethoate. Make sure to check your plants regularly and spray when needed. Always follow the recommended doses and rotate insecticides to avoid resistance.

My mustard plants are getting lots of aphids, what can I do?

Aphids are a big problem for mustard, especially in cold and cloudy weather. They suck the sap from leaves, buds, and pods, making the plants small and the seeds not grow well. You can use yellow sticky traps to monitor them. If there are 50-60 aphids on a 10 cm part of the plant or 40-50% of the plants are infested, you should spray. Use insecticides like thiamethoxam, oxydemeton-methyl, dimethoate, or chlorpyrifos. Spray in the early morning or late evening. Also, plant early and use resistant varieties to help prevent the problem.

How do I know when to spray for aphids in my mustard crop?

Use insecticides like thiamethoxam, oxydemeton-methyl, dimethoate, or chlorpyrifos. Spray in the morning or evening for best results.

My paddy plants are really short, what can I do to fix it?

You can try a few things. First, use the PAU leaf color chart to check if your plants need urea. Apply 25 kg of urea per acre if the leaves are lighter than shade 4 on the chart. Keep checking every 7 days, starting 14 days after transplanting. Make sure you have 2-3 inches of water in the field. Test the soil pH and add lime if it's too acidic. Also, check for zinc deficiency and apply zinc sulfate if needed. Use good quality seeds and plant them 20 x 15 cm apart. Add farmyard manure before transplanting. Watch for pests like stem borer and spray if needed. Don't use old seedlings or too much herbicide.

My berseem crop is getting some kind of disease, and the stems are rotting. What can I do about it?

The disease is called stem rot, and it's caused by a fungus. It usually shows up in January and February. The fungus lives in the soil as hard black seeds and attacks the stem, making it rot. You can see white, cottony growth around the affected plants. To control it, flood the field heavily in summer to kill the fungus. After cutting the crop, let the soil dry in the sun. Also, don't plant berseem in the same field for 3–4 years. Keep checking your fields regularly so you can act quickly if you see any signs.

After cutting the crop, let the soil dry in the sun. Don't plant berseem in the same field for 3–4 years. Check the fields often, especially in winter, and act fast when you see signs of the disease.

My paddy seedlings are getting wet every morning after I water them in the evening. Is that bad for them?

My wheat crop is getting damaged by some worms, what can I do?

If the problem gets worse, you might need to use insecticides like Chlorantraniliprole or Fipronil. Make sure to follow the dosage instructions carefully.

How far apart should I plant my potato seeds?

Smaller or earlier varieties can be closer, while bigger ones need more space.

My paddy plants are showing yellow leaves, what can I do?

I want to transplant my gobhi sarson. When should I do it and how?

You should transplant gobhi sarson in November for the best results. First, raise a nursery 30 days before transplanting. Use 8 marlas of land for one acre. Mix 4.5 kg urea and 4 kg of super phosphate into the soil. Sow 400 g of seed and water it after 10 days. Then, transplant the seedlings into furrows 45 cm apart, placing one every 10-15 cm. You can also use raised beds for better yield and water savings. Water right after transplanting and apply the rest of the nitrogen 3-4 weeks later.

How do I transplant Gobhi Sarson in Punjab for better yield?

You can also use raised beds for higher yield and water savings. Broadcast half the nitrogen and all the phosphorus before planting. My potato plants are getting all black and wet, and I'm losing my whole crop. What can I do?

Late blight is a big problem for potatoes. It starts with wet spots on the leaves that turn black and have a white fungus. The best way to stop it is to use healthy seed tubers and bury any infected ones. You should also plant short-duration varieties like Kufri Chipsona-1 or Kufri Frysona. If you spray your crop with Propineb, Mancozeb, or Chlorothalonil in early November, it can help prevent the disease. Make sure to mix the chemicals in enough water and spray them properly.

What is Panchagavya and how do I use it for my paddy crop?

Panchagavya is a mix of five cow products like cow dung, urine, milk, curd, and ghee. It’s used in organic farming to help plants grow better. You can use it as a seed treatment, foliar spray, or soil drench. For paddy, mix 1% Panchagavya with water to soak seeds overnight. For spraying, use 3% solution. It helps improve growth and make plants stronger. You can also add it to the soil around the roots. Some farmers use it at 30, 45, and 60 days after sowing. It’s a natural way to boost plant health and soil quality.

How do I make Panchagavya for my paddy field?

To make Panchagavya, take 7 kg of cow dung and 1 kg of ghee. Mix them well in a pot for 3 days, morning and evening. After that, add 10 liters of cow urine and 10 liters of water. Keep stirring twice a day. Let it ferment for 15 to 30 days. You can also add some jaggery, banana, or coconut water to help the microbes grow. Once ready, filter it and use it for your paddy. You can spray it on the leaves or use it as a seed treatment.

My wheat plants are getting damaged by insects, what can I do?

Termites can harm your wheat after sowing and near maturity. To stop them, plough deeply in summer, use well-rotted manure, and avoid sowing too late. You can treat seeds with Chlorpyriphos 20% EC or Thiamethoxam 70% WS. Neem cake also helps. If termites are bad, use Fipronil 0.30% GR or Chlorpyriphos 20% EC. Aphids suck sap, so use yellow traps and bird perches. If they get too bad, spray Imidacloprid, Thiamethoxam, or Quinalphos. Pink stem borer damages young plants, so keep irrigation regular and spray Quinalphos when you see symptoms.

If they get too bad, spray Imidacloprid, Thiamethoxam, or Quinalphos. For pink stem borer, spray Quinalphos when you see the first signs of damage. Keeping the field well-irrigated also helps reduce pest problems.

How much DAP should I use for my basmati paddy nursery?

You should use 8-10 kg of DAP per acre for your basmati paddy nursery. This gives enough nitrogen and phosphorus to help the seedlings grow strong. Apply all the DAP 1-2 days before sowing. Make sure the soil is well-prepared and watered properly. You can also add some zinc sulfate and farmyard manure for extra nutrients. Always wash your hands after handling the fertilizer.

Can I mix Cartap Hydrochloride granules with urea when I'm trying to control stem borers in my paddy field?

No, you shouldn't mix Cartap Hydrochloride granules with urea. They need to be applied separately. Cartap is for controlling stem borers, and urea is for fertilizing. Mixing them can make both less effective. Apply Cartap Hydrochloride 4% granules at 18.75 kg per hectare in standing water during the tillering to early booting stage. Don't mix it with urea. Always follow the instructions carefully.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
Kya main paddy mein stem borer ko control karne ke liye cartap hydrochloride granules ko urea ke saath mila sakta hoon?

Nahi, cartap hydrochloride granules ko urea ke saath mila ke nahi karna chahiye. Dono cheezon ko alag-alag apply karna chahiye taki unka karya kshamta kharab na ho. Cartap hydrochloride 4% granules ko 18.75 kg per hectare ki doshi se pani mein daalna chahiye. Yeh 5-7 cm pani ke depth wale field mein tillering se early booting stage tak apply karna hota hai. Pani ko 2-3 din baad bhi rakhein. Yeh 7-10 din ke andar stem borer ko control karta hai.

Can I mix cartap hydrochloride granules with urea to kill stem borers in my paddy?

Mixing them might not work as well. Apply cartap at 18.75 kg per hectare in 5-7 cm of water during the right time. Wear gloves and a mask when handling it. Follow the instructions carefully.

What's the right way to use Cartap Hydrochloride for stem borer in paddy?

You should apply Cartap Hydrochloride 4% granules separately from urea. Don't mix them. Use 18.75 kg per hectare in standing water during the tillering to early booting stage. Broadcast the granules evenly and keep water in the field for 2-3 days. This helps the insecticide work better. If the infestation is bad, you might need to use it. Also, wear protective gear when applying it. Make sure to follow the dosage and safety instructions carefully.

My paddy plants are showing dry and wilting tillers, what could be causing this?

The dry and wilting tillers in your paddy could be due to stem borer infestation. The larvae of the yellow stem borer bore into the stems, causing these symptoms. It's important to apply Cartap Hydrochloride 4% granules at 18.75 kg per hectare in standing water during the tillering to early booting stage. Do not mix it with urea, as this can reduce its effectiveness. Use proper safety gear when handling the granules and follow the recommended application method.

What's the deal with cotton bolls opening? I've seen some that don't open properly.

Boll opening in cotton is when the boll cracks open to release the lint. It's controlled by hormones. Ethylene helps the boll open, while auxin keeps it closed when it's still young. As the boll matures, auxin goes down and ethylene goes up, making the boll open. If the boll isn't mature enough or the plant is stressed, the boll might not open right. Keeping the plant healthy with proper water, nutrients, and pest control helps the bolls open properly.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
What's the deal with cotton bolls opening? How does that work?

Cotton bolls open when the plant releases a hormone called ethylene. Before that, the bolls have another hormone called auxin that keeps them closed. As the boll matures, auxin goes down and ethylene goes up, which makes the boll start to open. The boll has a weak spot, and when it's ready, it cracks open. If the plant is stressed or not healthy, the boll might not open right. So keeping the plant healthy with enough water and nutrients is important for the bolls to open properly.
You are an agricultural advisory assistant helping farmers with crop management.
How do cotton bolls open, and what makes them open properly?

Cotton bolls open because of a hormone called ethylene. When the boll is mature, ethylene levels go up, which makes the boll start to open. Inside the boll, there's a weak line that breaks down when it's ready. This causes the boll to crack open and show the lint. But if the boll isn't healthy, or if there's not enough water or nutrients, it might not open right. So, keeping the plants healthy with proper water and care helps the bolls open properly.

How do cotton bolls open, and what causes them to do that?

Cotton bolls open because of a hormone called ethylene. When the boll is young, another hormone called auxin stops it from opening too soon. As the boll gets mature, auxin goes down and ethylene goes up. This makes the boll start to crack open. The boll has a weak line inside, and when it dries, the outer covering splits. But if the boll isn't healthy, or if there's not enough water, it might not open right. Good care, like proper water and nutrients, helps bolls open properly.

What causes cotton bolls to open, and how does it happen?

Cotton bolls open because of a hormone called ethylene. When the boll is young, another hormone called auxin stops it from opening too soon. As the boll matures, auxin decreases and ethylene increases, which makes the boll start to open. Inside the boll, there's a weak line called a suture. When it's ready, that line breaks down, and the boll cracks open. The boll dries, and the outer covering splits, showing the lint. If the boll isn't healthy or gets too dry, it might not open right. Good care, like proper water and nutrition, helps the bolls open properly.

At 45 days, weeds can really hurt your wheat crop. You should do manual weeding with a khurpi or wheel hoe. It helps remove weeds without chemicals and makes the soil better. Do it when the soil is wet, like after watering. You can also spray chemicals like Isoproturon or Sulfosulfuron to get rid of grassy weeds. For broadleaf weeds, use 2,4-D or Carfentrazone. Make sure to spray evenly and follow the right doses. Doing this now will help your wheat grow better.

How deep should I plant the maize seeds for good fodder growth?

Keep the rows 30 cm apart and use around 30 kg of seeds per acre. You can plant from March to September, but it's better to plant between April and August to avoid pests. You can grow up to three fodder crops a year. You can also plant using traditional methods or no-till farming.

What is waste decomposer and how can it help me with my paddy field?

Waste decomposer is a special mix of microbes that helps break down paddy straw into useful compost. It’s made by a place in New Delhi and helps farmers use less chemical fertilizer. You can use it in capsule or liquid form. To make the liquid, you mix jaggery, besan, and the capsules in water. After a few days, it’s ready to spray on your field. This helps break down the straw faster and makes the soil better for the next crop. You need about 5 liters for every 10 quintals of straw.

My wheat plants are getting some kind of disease, and I can't tell what it is. What should I do?

Wheat can get diseases like rusts, powdery mildew, and karnal bunt, as well as pests like aphids and termites. Some diseases, like karnal bunt, are hard to spot in the field but can be seen after harvest. To manage this, use proper farming methods like good drainage, crop rotation, and resistant varieties. You can also spray chemicals like propiconazole or thiram. For pests, use neem cake or chemical treatments like chlorpyrifos. Keeping your field clean and checking regularly helps too.

What pests and diseases harm my wheat crop and how can I manage them?

Your wheat can be affected by pests like aphids, armyworms, termites, and weevils, as well as diseases like rusts, powdery mildew, and Karnal bunt. For rust, you can use Propiconazole or Tebuconazole. To control termites, apply neem cake or treat seeds with Thiamethoxam. For aphids, use Quinalphos or Thiamethoxam. Cultural methods like proper spacing, crop rotation, and avoiding too much nitrogen help too. You can also use sticky traps for aphids and burn stubble after harvest to reduce disease spread.

What are the best ways to stop my wheat from getting rust?

To stop rust in wheat, use resistant varieties like HD 2501 or MACS 2496. Avoid too much nitrogen, and spray Propiconazole 25% EC or Tebuconazole 25% WG. Also, rotate crops and don't plant wheat in the same field every year. Keep an eye on the weather, as rust likes cool and humid conditions.

My onions are getting messed up by tiny bugs, what’s going on and how do I fix it?

The tiny bugs on your onions are mites. Three types are common: red spider mites, eriophyid mites, and bulb mites. Red spider mites leave small dots on leaves and make them look pale. They also make webs. Eriophyid mites cause leaves to twist and turn yellow. Bulb mites get into the onions and make them rot. To manage them, spray the plants with water to wash the mites off. Also, keep the soil wet with flooding. Try to grow different plants after onions, like marigold or clover. Help natural enemies like ladybugs and lacewings by not using strong chemicals.

My maize plants are not growing well, and the leaves are getting purple and yellow. What's wrong?

It sounds like your maize might be lacking phosphorus and potassium. Phosphorus deficiency causes older leaves to turn purple and stunts growth, while potassium deficiency makes the leaf edges turn yellow and brown. To fix this, add 10 tonnes of well-decomposed manure per acre before planting. You can also use fertilizers like SSP or DAP for phosphorus and MOP for potassium. For sandy loam soils, use 100 kg SSP or 35 kg DAP, plus 15 kg MOP and 70 kg urea. For sandy soils, cut these amounts in half. Always check your soil test before applying fertilizers to get the best results.

What should I do to make sure my fodder maize gets enough phosphorus and potassium?

To ensure your fodder maize gets enough phosphorus and potassium, use well-decomposed farmyard manure at 10 tonnes per acre before planting. This helps supply most of the phosphorus and potassium needed. You can also apply chemical fertilizers like SSP or DAP along with MOP and urea. For sandy loam to clay loam soils, use 100 kg SSP or 35 kg DAP, 15 kg MOP, and 70 kg urea per acre. For loamy sand to sandy soils, use 50 kg SSP or 18 kg DAP, 8 kg MOP, and 35 kg urea. If using DAP, reduce urea by 15 kg or 8 kg depending on the amount. Drill half the nitrogen and all the phosphorus and potassium at sowing. Apply the rest of the nitrogen after 3-4 weeks. Always check your soil test report before applying fertilizers to avoid overuse and ensure better yield.

What biofertilizers can I use for my potato crop and how do I apply them?

You can use a consortium biofertilizer for your potato crop. Apply 4 kg of it per acre mixed with 10 kg of manure or soil and spread it in the field. The biofertilizer has Azotobacter, Bacillus, and Pseudomonas. You can also use Biozyme granules—8 kg per acre at planting and earthing up. Spray Biozyme liquid at tuber initiation, 200 ml per acre. Using biofertilizers helps improve soil and boost yield.
